steps.title: Identify the grammatical structures
steps.content: On the left: “Scott-Heron himself resisted the godfather” forms a complete independent clause (once “nickname” is added as the object). On the right: “feeling that it didn't encapsulate his devotion...” is a participial phrase.

steps.title: Apply the parenthetical punctuation rule
steps.content: Parenthetical words that interrupt a sentence must be enclosed by commas on both sides, preserving the unity of the sentence. Option C correctly places commas around “however,” to enclose it as a parenthetical interrupter. Options A, B, and D all fail this rule: option A lacks the comma after “however,” option B uses an illogical semicolon mid-punctuation, and option D uses a semicolon to incorrectly treat the following participial phrase as a second independent clause. That matches choice C — C is correct.
